contents:
Have you ever felt you don’t deserve your achievements and that, at any moment, someone will discover you’re “not that capable”? That feeling has a name: impostor syndrome. It’s not a disorder, but a thinking pattern that leads competent people to doubt themselves and attribute their success to luck or external factors.
It often appears when we step outside our comfort zone: a new job, a professional challenge, an important project, or even personal changes. Instead of recognizing growth, the mind interprets novelty as proof of inadequacy.
Common signs Minimizing achievements. Constant comparison with others. Feeling you must know everything before acting. Excessive fear of making mistakes. What helps manage it Reviewing real evidence of your abilities. Talking openly about your doubts. Accepting that learning involves not knowing. Treating yourself with the same understanding you offer others. Coaching as an ally Coaching can be a powerful tool for addressing impostor syndrome because it provides a safe, structured space to observe your thoughts without judgment and question the beliefs behind them. Through powerful questions and guided reflection, it helps distinguish facts from interpretations, recognize inner resources, and build a more realistic and empowering self-narrative.
The process doesn’t stop at awareness—it moves into action. Designing small steps, validating progress, and sustaining commitment allows confidence to become an experience, not just an idea.
Impostor syndrome doesn’t mean you’re not capable; often it means you’re growing. And growth almost always feels uncomfortable before it feels natural.
title: Impostor Syndrome: When You Doubt Yourself Even Though You're Doing Well

contents:
Leadership is changing. Today, organizations need leaders who can generate trust, commitment, and sustainable results. In this new context, positive leadership has become one of the most valued competencies in companies and organizations. But what exactly is it, and why does it make such a difference?
What is positive leadership? Positive leadership is a way of leading based on trust, conscious communication, and the development of people’s potential. A positive leader:
listens inspires builds commitment develops talent creates healthy and productive work environments They do not lead through control or fear, but through clarity, coherence, and shared responsibility.
Why it is key in today’s organizations We live in complex, fast-changing, and demanding environments. Constant pressure leads to burnout, disconnection, and lack of motivation. Positive leadership brings very concrete benefits:
improves workplace climate increases team engagement reduces conflict and stress fosters collaboration and innovation drives sustainable results That is why more and more companies are investing in the development of this type of leadership.
The keys to positive leadership Self-awareness: Good leadership starts with yourself. Knowing yourself and understanding your impact is the foundation for leading with coherence. Conscious communication: Listening, dialoguing, and giving high-quality feedback strengthen relationships and improve results. Trust and responsibility: Trusting people and giving them space to grow generates real commitment. Meaning and purpose: Connecting work with a greater purpose increases motivation and involvement. Talent development: A positive leader does not only manage tasks: they develop people. The role of coaching in positive leadership Executive and leadership coaching is a key tool for developing more conscious, human, and effective leadership. Through coaching, leaders gain clarity, make better decisions, and strengthen their impact.
Leadership that leaves a mark Positive leadership transforms results, teams, and cultures. It is a more human, more effective, and more sustainable way of leading. If you want to develop more conscious and effective leadership, both personally and within your organization, I will be happy to support you.
title: Positive leadership: a way of leading that transforms people and organizations

contents:
In a world that moves increasingly fast, we tend to think that transformation means changing jobs, cities, or situations. However, true transformation does not begin outside, but within. It is not a geographical move nor an impulsive leap: it is a deep shift in how we observe reality and how we observe ourselves.
Perspective shapes how we interpret what happens to us, influences our decisions, and determines the direction we move in. That is why, when we change perspective, the entire experience changes.
Change vs. transformation: two different forces Change is usually external: new responsibilities, a new team, a new project, or even a new purpose. It is visible, measurable, concrete. Transformation, on the other hand, occurs at a deeper level. It has to do with:
how we think, how we feel, how we make decisions, and how we relate to our environment. We can change jobs without transforming. And we can also transform without changing jobs. The key lies in our inner gaze, in how we reinterpret our story, our abilities, and our possibilities.
The role of coaching in this process Coaching provides a safe space to explore perspectives, beliefs, and tendencies that sometimes act as invisible limits. Through this support, we:
gain clarity, recognize patterns that hold us back, connect with our resources, and discover alternatives we had not seen before. Coaching does not dictate the path: it helps you discover it. It allows the pieces to come together in a more conscious, aligned, and authentic way.
A simple story to illustrate it Imagine someone who feels they have reached a professional limit. They try changing roles, companies, or sectors… yet the feeling remains. In a coaching conversation, they discover that what limits them is not their job, but the belief that “I’m not ready,” a phrase they have silently repeated for years. The day they question that belief and look at it from a different perspective, the experience changes. The environment does not transform: the perspective does. And from that new perspective, decisions become freer and more coherent.
Transformation: a continuous process Transformation is not a final destination. It is a path in which we:
learn, unlearn, expand possibilities, and establish new ways of relating to our environment. It does not always involve major moves. Sometimes it is a small gesture, a deep reflection, or a new way of responding to everyday situations. But every micro-transformation adds up.
Conclusion When we talk about transformation, we are not talking about escaping, but about evolving. It is not about changing places, but about changing perspective, broadening our view to act with greater clarity, presence, and purpose. If you are in a moment of review, search, or change, it may be time to explore that inner perspective. Transformation begins there.
title: Transforming is not about changing places: it's about changing perspective

contents:
With more than 15 years of experience in consulting, coaching, and project management, I have supported organizations and individuals through profound change processes. My purpose is to foster human and organizational development, creating environments where innovation, collaboration, and learning become drivers of sustainable growth.
My approach integrates strategic clarity with active listening and empathy, enabling transformations that allow individuals and teams to overcome challenges, reinvent themselves, and achieve their most ambitious goals. Throughout my career, I have learned to read between the lines, to perceive what is not said, and to build genuine connections that generate trust and commitment.
I do not believe in instant solutions or ready-made formulas: I trust in the power of self-knowledge, strategy, and strong relationships as the foundations for driving meaningful and lasting change.
My Philosophy and Approach As a Coach I firmly believe in the transformative potential of every individual, which begins with a mindset shift and takes shape through conscious action. I work with listening, closeness, and genuine commitment to each process. I accompany individuals and teams as they discover their own path, enhancing their resources and generating sustainable change. My focus is clear: to grow from who we are, not from what we lack.
As a Change Manager I am driven by a deep passion for transformation, innovation, and excellence. My approach combines business strategy, organizational design, creativity, and agile methodologies to achieve transformations with real and lasting impact. I am motivated by the desire to challenge the conventional, simplify the complex, inspire teams, and build people-centered solutions that truly work.
My Professional Essence It combines the strategic clarity of a consultant with the deep listening of a coach. Throughout my career, I have supported complex transformation processes, facilitating cultural and organizational change with both a practical and human perspective. I help turn complexity into concrete actions while creating spaces for reflection that enable people and teams to move forward with purpose toward sustainable growth.

title: About Me
